concepts.title: System of equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Solution of simultaneous linear equations (Matrix Method)

Let us consider the following system of$ ext{n}$ linear equations in $ ext{n}$ unknowns ${{ ext{x}}_{1}},{{ ext{x}}_{2}},\ldots ,{{ ext{x}}_{ ext{n}}}$

$\left. egin{matrix} {{a}_{11}}{{x}_{1}}+{{a}_{12}}{{x}_{2}}+\ldots +{{a}_{1n}}{{x}_{n}}={{b}_{1}} \ {{a}_{21}}{{x}_{1}}+{{a}_{22}}{{x}_{2}}+\ldots +{{a}_{2n}}{{x}_{n}}={{b}_{2}} \ \ldots \quad \ldots \ \ldots \ {{a}_{n1}}{{x}_{1}}+{{a}_{n2}}{{x}_{2}}+\ldots +{{a}_{nn}}{{x}_{n}}={{b}_{n}} \ \end{matrix} ight\}$ … (1)

If ${{ ext{b}}_{1}}={{ ext{b}}_{2}}=\ldots ={{ ext{b}}_{ ext{n}}}=0$, then the system of equations (1) is called a system of homogeneous linear equations and if at least one of ${{b}_{1}},{{b}_{2}}\ldots ,{{b}_{n}}$ is non zero, then it is called a system of non homogeneous linear equations.

Solution of a system of equations:

A set of values ${{\alpha }_{1}},{{\alpha }_{2}},\ldots ,{{\alpha }_{n}}$ of ${{x}_{1}},{{x}_{2}},\ldots ,{{x}_{n}}$ respectively which satisfy all the equations of the given system of equations is called a solution of the given system of equations.

The system of equations is said to be consistent if its solution exist otherwise it is said to be inconsistent.

$ ext{ Let }\quad ext{A}=\left[ egin{matrix} {{a}_{11}} & {{a}_{12}} & \ldots & {{a}_{1n}} \ {{a}_{21}} & {{a}_{22}} & \ldots & {{a}_{2n}} \ \vdots & \vdots & \ldots & \vdots \ {{a}_{n1}} & {{a}_{n2}} & \ldots & {{a}_{nn}} \ \end{matrix} ight],X=\left[ egin{matrix} {{x}_{1}} \ {{x}_{2}} \ \vdots \ {{b}_{n}} \ \end{matrix} ight],B=\left[ egin{matrix} {{b}_{1}} \ {{b}_{2}} \ \vdots \ {{b}_{n}} \ \end{matrix} ight]$

Then the system of equations (1) can be written as$ ext{AX}= ext{B}$

Here $ ext{A}$ is a $ ext{n}$ matrix and is called the coefficient matrix. Each of $ ext{X}$ and $ ext{B}$ is a $ ext{n}\quad 1$ matrix.

I. When system of equations is non-homogeneous: 

(i) If $| ext{A}| e 0$, the system of equations is consistent and has a unique solution given by $ ext{X}={{ ext{A}}^{-1}}~ ext{B}$

Proof: Since $|A| e 0$, therefore ${{A}^{-1}}$ exist.

Now $AX=B ackslash {{A}^{-1}}(AX)={{A}^{-1}}B$

or, $\quad \left( {{A}^{-1}}A ight)X={{A}^{-1}}B$ [by associative law for matrix multiplication]

or $\quad {{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}} ext{X}={{ ext{A}}^{-1}}~ ext{B}$

or $ ext{X}={{ ext{A}}^{-1}}~ ext{B}\left[ herefore {{ ext{I}}_{ ext{n}}} ext{X}= ext{X} ight]$

(ii) If $| ext{A}|=0$, the system of equations has no solution or an infinite number of solutions according as (adj A).B is non-zero or zero.

II. When system of equations is homogeneous: 

(i) If $| ext{A}| e 0$, the system of equations has only trivial solution and number of solutions is one.

(ii) If $| ext{A}|=0$, the system of equations has non-trivial solution and the number of solutions is infinite.

If the system of homogeneous linear equations has number of equations less than the number of unknowns, then it has non-trivial solution.

concepts.title: Representation of equations in matrix form
concepts.conceptBody:
Solution of Equations

We have already introduced the following terms in relation to solution of equations, i.e. Consistent (having solution). Inconsistent (having no solution), unique (only one solution) infinite (many solutions). Trivial (all variables zero i.e. unique.

Representation of equations in matrix form

I. Intersecting lines. Unique solution. Consistent

$x+2y=4$

$3x+y=2$

or $\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{x} \\ \text{y} \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 \\ 2 \\ \end{array} \right]$ or $\text{AX}=\text{B}$

A is called coefficient matrix and $\text{C}=[\text{A},\text{B}]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 1 & 2 \\ \end{array} \right]$ is $2\quad 3$ matrix. $\text{C}$ is called augmented matrix.

Nature of solution

(a) Rank $\text{A}=\operatorname{Rank}\text{C}=\text{n}=2$ the number of unknown. Solution is unique and is obtained as under.

Here $|A|=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]=1-6=-5\neq 0$

$\therefore$ A is non-singular and ${{\text{A}}^{-1}}$ exists and

${{\text{A}}^{-1}}=\frac{1}{|~\text{A}|}(\operatorname{adj}\cdot \text{A})=\frac{1}{-5}\left[ \begin{matrix} 1 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Multiplying both sides by ${{\text{A}}^{-1}}$ we have

${{\text{A}}^{-1}}(\text{AX})={{\text{A}}^{-1}}~\text{B or }\quad \text{X}={{\text{A}}^{-1}}~\text{B}$

or $\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{x} \\ \text{y} \\ \end{array} \right]=-\frac{1}{5}\left[ \begin{matrix} 1 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 \\ 2 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$=-\frac{1}{5}\left[ \begin{matrix} 0 \\ -10 \\ \end{matrix} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 \\ 2 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$\therefore \quad \text{x }=0$

$\text{y }=2$

2nd Method by Cramer’s rule

$\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{D}}_{1}}}=\frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{D}}_{2}}}=\frac{1}{\text{D}}$

$\text{ or }\quad \frac{\text{x}}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right|}=\frac{\text{y}}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 4 \\ 3 & 2 \\ \end{array} \right|}=\frac{1}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right|}\quad \text{ ar }\quad \frac{\text{x}}{0}=\frac{\text{y}}{-10}=\frac{1}{-5}$

(b) Homogeneous equations. Unique solutions. Trivial.

$x+2y=0$

$3x+2y=0\quad \text{ or }\quad \left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} x \\ y \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 \\ 0 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$AX=B$

$\text{C}=[\text{A},\text{B}]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right]$

Here as above Rank $\text{A}=\operatorname{Rank}\text{C}=\text{n}=2$ the number of unknown variables and hence the solution will be unique and is given by $\text{X}={{\text{A}}^{-1}}~\text{B}$

$\text{ or }\quad \left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{x} \\ \text{y} \\ \end{array} \right]=-\frac{1}{\mathbf{5}}\left[ \begin{matrix} 1 & -2 \\ -3 & 1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 \\ 0 \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 \\ 0 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$\therefore \quad \text{x }=0\text{y }=2$

$\frac{\text{x}}{{{\text{D}}_{1}}}=\frac{\text{y}}{{{\text{D}}_{2}}}=\frac{1}{\text{D}}$

or $\frac{\text{x}}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right|}=\frac{\text{y}}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 4 \\ 3 & 2 \\ \end{array} \right|}=\frac{1}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right|}$

or $\frac{\text{x}}{0}=\frac{\text{y}}{-10}=\frac{1}{-5}$

II Coincident lines. Infinite solutions. Consistent 

(c) x + 2y = 4

3x + 6y = 12

or $\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{x} \\ \text{y} \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{matrix} 4 \\ 12 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

or $\text{AX}=\text{B}$ $\text{C}=[\text{A},\text{B}]=\left[ \begin{matrix} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 & 12 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Here $|A|=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]=0$

so that matrix $\text{A}$ is singular and ${{\text{A}}^{-1}}$ will not exist Apply ${{R}_{2}}-3{{R}_{1}}$

$C=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right]\text{ and }A=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 \\ \end{array} \right]$

Clearly Rank $\text{C}=$ Rank $\text{A}=1$ which is less than $\text{n}=2$ the number of variables and hence the system of equations are consistent and will have infinite solutions. As a matter of fact we have only one equation on two variables $\text{x}+2\text{y}=4$ (The second equation on dividing by 3 becomes the same as $x+2y=4$ )

$\therefore \quad$ We can have infinite number of points on a line Choosing $\text{x}=\text{c}$ we have $\text{y}=\frac{4-\text{c}}{2}$

(d) Homogeneous equations 

$\text{x}+2\text{y}=0$

$3x+6y=0\text{ or }\quad \left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} x \\ y \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 0 \\ 0 \\ \end{array} \right]$

or $\quad \text{Ax}=\text{B}$

Arguing as in (c) Rank $\text{C}=\operatorname{Rank}\text{A}=1,|~\text{A}|=0$. As a matter of fact we have only one equation $\text{x}+2\text{y}=0$. Rank $\text{C}$ = Rank $\text{A}=1,<\text{n}=2$ the number of variables.

Choosing $\text{y}=\text{c},\text{x}=-2\text{c}\therefore (\text{c},-2\text{c})$ constitute infinite solutions.

III. Parallel lines. Inconsistent. No solution.

(e) $x+2y=4$

$3x+6y=7\quad \text{ or }\quad \left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 \\ 7 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$\text{or }\quad \text{AX}=\text{B}\quad \text{C}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 & 7 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$|A|=0$ i.e., $A$ is singular.

Clearly Rank $\text{A}=1$ but Rank $\text{C}=2$

Rank A < Rank C $\therefore $ Inconsistent

As a matter of fact the two equations are $x+2y=4$ and $x+2y=\frac{7}{3}$ which are clearly inconsistent and have no solution.

Gist

I $\operatorname{Rank }\text{A}=\operatorname{Rank }\text{C}=\text{n}$ the number of variables then equations are consistent and have unique solution. $\text{A}\ne 0$ sec (a), (b).

II Rank $\text{A}=\operatorname{Rank }\text{C}=\text{r}<\text{n}$ the number of variables the equations are consistentand have infinite solutions, $|\text{A}|=0\text{sec}(\text{c})$, (d).

III $\operatorname{Rank }A<\operatorname{Rank }C$

$\therefore \quad $ Inconsistent, $|\text{A}|=0\sec (\text{e})$.

Non-homogeneous equation : We will illustrate the method by two examples. We have discussed this topic in determinants. Here we illustrate the method by use of matrices.

concepts.title: Cramer's Rule
concepts.conceptBody:
System of liner Equations

Definition 1:

A system of linear equations in $n$ unknowns ${{x}_{1}},{{x}_{2}},{{x}_{3}},\ldots .{{x}_{n}}$ is of the form:

$\left\{ \begin{align} & {{a}_{11}}{{x}_{1}}+{{a}_{12}}{{x}_{2}}+.....+{{a}_{1n}}{{x}_{n}}={{b}_{1}} \\ & {{a}_{21}}{{x}_{1}}+{{a}_{22}}{{x}_{2}}+.....+{{a}_{2n}}{{x}_{n}}={{b}_{2}} \\ & ................................................... \\ & ................................................... \\ & {{a}_{n1}}{{x}_{1}}+{{a}_{n2}}{{x}_{2}}+.....+{{a}_{nn}}{{x}_{n}}={{b}_{n}} \\ \end{align} \right\}.....(A)$

If ${{\text{b}}_{1}},~{{\text{b}}_{2}},\ldots ..{{\text{b}}_{\text{n}}}$ are all zero, the system is called homogeneous and non-homogeneous if at least one ${{b}_{i}}$ is non-zero.

Definition 2:

The solution set of the system (A) is an $n$ tuple $\left( {{\alpha }_{1}},{{\alpha }_{2}},\ldots .,{{\alpha }_{n}} \right)$ of real numbers (or complex numbers if the coefficients are complex) which satisfy each of the equations of the system.

Definition 3. 

A system of equations is called consistent if it has at least one solution; inconsistent if it does not have any solution; determinate if it has a unique solution; indeterminate if it has more than one solution.

Gist of discussion in simple language:

1. Consistent: Solution exists whether unique or infinite number of solutions.

2. Inconsistent: Solution does not exist.

3. Homogeneous Equations: Constant terms zero.

4. Trivial solution: All variables zero i.e., $\text{x}=0,\text{y}=0,\text{z}=0$

5. Non-trivial Solution: Infinite number of solutions.

As a matter of fact on division by 2 the second equation reduces to first. Thus we have got only one line $2x+3y=10$ on which lie infinite number of points. Thus there are infinite number of solutions and the system is consistent.

Solution of System of Linear Equations by Determinants (Cramer’s Rule)

System of linear equations in two variables:

Consider the system ${{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y={{c}_{1}}$

${{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y={{c}_{2}}$

Solving these equations, we get

$x=\frac{{{b}_{2}}{{c}_{1}}-{{b}_{1}}{{c}_{2}}}{{{a}_{1}}{{b}_{2}}-{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{1}}},\quad y=\frac{{{a}_{1}}{{c}_{2}}-{{a}_{2}}{{c}_{1}}}{{{a}_{1}}{{b}_{2}}-{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{1}}}$

These can be expressed in terms of determinants as:

$\text{x}=\frac{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{c}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{c}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|}$ $\text{y}=\frac{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|}{\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|}$

$\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{{{D}_{x}}}{D},y=\frac{{{D}_{y}}}{D}\text{ where }D=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{1}} & {{b}_{1}} \\ {{a}_{2}} & {{b}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|\text{ is the determinant of coefficient of }x\text{ and }y$

${{D}_{x}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{c}_{1}} & {{b}_{1}} \\ {{c}_{2}} & {{b}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|\text{ is obtained by replacing coefficient of }x\text{ by constants }{{c}_{1}},{{c}_{2}}\text{ in }D\text{ and }$

${{\text{D}}_{\text{y}}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ \end{array} \right|\text{ is obtained by replacing coefficient of y by constants }{{\text{c}}_{1}},{{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{ in D}\text{. }$

System of linear equations in three variables: 

Consider the system ${{a}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{1}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}\text{z}={{\text{d}}_{1}}$

${{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{z}={{\text{d}}_{2}}$

${{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{3}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{3}}\text{z}={{\text{d}}_{3}}$

Let $\text{D}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{3}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{3}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|$

Then, $xD=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{1}}x & {{b}_{1}} & {{c}_{1}} \\ {{a}_{2}}x & {{b}_{2}} & {{c}_{2}} \\ {{a}_{3}}x & {{b}_{3}} & {{c}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|$

Applying ${{\text{C}}_{1}}\to {{\text{C}}_{1}}+\text{y}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+\text{z}{{\text{C}}_{3}}$

$\text{xD}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{1}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}\text{z} & {{\text{b}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}\text{z} & {{\text{b}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{3}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{3}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{3}}\text{z} & {{\text{b}}_{3}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|$$=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{d}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ ~{{\text{d}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ ~{{\text{d}}_{3}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{3}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|={{\text{D}}_{\text{x}}}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \text{x}=\frac{\text{Dx}}{\text{D}}$

Similarly, $\quad y=\frac{{{D}_{y}}}{D}$ and $\quad z=\frac{{{D}_{z}}}{D}$

where, $D=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{a}_{1}} & {{b}_{1}} & {{c}_{1}} \\ {{a}_{2}} & {{b}_{2}} & {{c}_{2}} \\ {{a}_{3}} & {{b}_{3}} & {{c}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|\text{,}$is the determinant of the coefficient of x, y and z.

${{\text{D}}_{\text{x}}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{d}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ ~{{\text{d}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ ~{{\text{d}}_{3}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{3}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|$

is obtained by replacing coefficients of $x$ by constants ${{d}_{1}},{{d}_{2}},{{d}_{3}}$ in $D$.

${{\text{D}}_{\text{y}}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{d}}_{1}} & {{\text{c}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{d}}_{2}} & {{\text{c}}_{2}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{3}} & ~{{\text{d}}_{3}} & {{\text{c}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|$

is obtained by replacing coefficient of y by constants ${{\text{d}}_{1}},~{{\text{d}}_{2}},~{{\text{d}}_{3}}\text{ in D}\text{. }$

${{\text{D}}_{\text{z}}}=\left| \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {{\text{a}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{1}} & ~{{\text{d}}_{1}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{2}} & ~{{\text{d}}_{2}} \\ {{\text{a}}_{3}} & ~{{\text{b}}_{3}} & ~{{\text{d}}_{3}} \\ \end{array} \right|$

is obtained by replacing coefficients of $\text{z}$ by constants ${{\text{d}}_{1}},~{{\text{d}}_{2}},~{{\text{d}}_{3}}$ in $\text{D}$.

Criterion for consistency

For system of lienar equations in two variables:

(i) If $\text{D}\ne 0$, then the system is consistent and has a unique solution.

(ii) If $\text{D}=0,{{\text{D}}_{\text{x}}}=0$ and ${{\text{D}}_{\text{y}}}=0$ then the system is consistent and has infinite number of solutions.

(iii) If $\text{D}=0$ and any one of ${{\text{D}}_{\text{x}}}$ or ${{\text{D}}_{\text{y}}}$ is non-zero, then the system is inconsistent (no solutions).

For system of lienar equations in three variables:

(i) If $\text{D}\ne 0$, then the system is consistent and has a unique solution.

(ii) If $\text{D}=0,{{\text{D}}_{\text{x}}}=0$ and ${{\text{D}}_{\text{y}}}=0$ and ${{\text{D}}_{\text{z}}}=0$, then the system is consistent and has infinite number of solutions.

(iii) If $\text{D}=0$ and any one of ${{\text{D}}_{\text{x}}},{{\text{D}}_{\text{y}}}$ or ${{\text{D}}_{\text{z}}}$ is non-zero, then the system is inconsistent.
